coredata.dc:title: Highly stable Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3perovskite nanoparticles as a new fluorescence nanosensor for selective detection of trace tetracycline in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Thuy, Truong Thi
Huy, Bui The
Kumar, Avvaru Praveen
Lee, Yong-Ill
coredata.dc:description: The overuse of antibiotics increases resistant bacteria, and their accumulation in the human body resulting in a pernicious influence or allergic effect on human health. As antibiotics have been used in livestock, their residue in food products is a serious problem. Therefore, the determination of tetracycline (TC) – an antibiotic in food samples by a simple, highly sensitive technique is necessary. This report presents a fluorescence sensor to detect TC based on fluorescence quenching by inner filter effect (IFE) between TC and Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 perovskite material. Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 acts as a fluorescence source with high luminescence quantum yield, narrow emission peak, and short radiative lifetime. It is also protected by perofluorooctyltriethyloxylsilane fluorocarbons assembled on the particle surface to form a water-resistance layer due to the high hydrophobic groups, –CF2– and –CF3, resulting in high aqueous dispersion. Under optimized conditions, the proposed sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity to detect TC in food samples. The limit of detection is calculated as 76 nM with a linear range of 0.4 – 10 µM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is assigned to IFE based on the excitation spectrum of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 and the absorption spectrum of TC, as well as the compensatory fluorescence intensity of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 with the energy absorbed by TC concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracycline
Foods
Paper-based sensor
Perovskite
Fluorescence

We greatly acknowledge Dr. Weon-Sik Chae in Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI) for fluorescence lifetime measurements and discussion on quenching mechanism.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of the prepared CPB-PFOS. The scale bar is 1 μm; (b) Distribution of the particle sizes measured from the SEM image using ImageJ software, (c) Emission spectra with different excitation wavelengths from 290 nm to 450 nm and excitation spectra (red line) of the prepared CPB-PFOS monitored at 521 nm, and (d) Absorption spectra of CPB-PFOS and TC sample.

(a) Response of FL intensity of different concentrations of CPB-PFOS aqueous solution in the presence of 25 µM of TC, where F and F0 are FL intensity with and without TC, respectively; (b) The spectra of aqueous solution of 5% (v/v) of CPB-PFOS samples with different concentrations of TC under an excitation of 355 nm wavelength; and (c) Relationship between TC concentration and FL intensity log((F0-F)/F0) = 3.281 + 0.7259*log(CTC(M)) with R2 = 0.986.

(a) Overlapping between excitation spectrum of the prepared CPB-PFOS and absorption spectrum of TC. No overlap of the emission spectrum of CPB-PFOS with the absorption spectrum of TC; and (b) Quenching efficiency of observed and corrected data for CPB-PFOS with different concentrations of TC in range of 0.4 – 100 µM.

(a) The response signal change (F0-F)/F0 of interferences in the presence/ absence of TC under an excitation wavelength of 355 nm; (b) Effect of the metal ions on (F0-F)/F0; (c) Effect of ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, ampicillin, and nitrofurantoin at 50 µM on FL intensity of CPB-PFOS in the presence (red bar) and absent (green bar) of TC; and (d) Absorption spectra of TC, ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, ampicillin, and nitrofurantoin exhibit high inner filter effect of TC at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm.
Color change of the paper-based CPB-PFOS sensor with different concentrations of TC which can be seen by naked eyes under UV light.
Analytical performance of the proposed TC method comparing with those of previous methods.
Determination of TC in real food samples by using the prepared CPB-PFOS sensor.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The 95 % confidence interval was calculated with Student’s t = 2.776 (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Highly stable Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3perovskite nanoparticles as a new fluorescence nanosensor for selective detection of trace tetracycline in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changwon National University, Changwon 51140, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changwon National University, Changwon 51140, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, School of Applied Natural Science, Adama Science and Technology University, PO Box 1888, Adama, Ethiopia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, School of Applied Natural Science, Adama Science and Technology University, PO Box 1888, Adama, Ethiopia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The overuse of antibiotics increases resistant bacteria, and their accumulation in the human body resulting in a pernicious influence or allergic effect on human health. As antibiotics have been used in livestock, their residue in food products is a serious problem. Therefore, the determination of tetracycline (TC) – an antibiotic in food samples by a simple, highly sensitive technique is necessary. This report presents a fluorescence sensor to detect TC based on fluorescence quenching by inner filter effect (IFE) between TC and Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 perovskite material. Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 acts as a fluorescence source with high luminescence quantum yield, narrow emission peak, and short radiative lifetime. It is also protected by perofluorooctyltriethyloxylsilane fluorocarbons assembled on the particle surface to form a water-resistance layer due to the high hydrophobic groups, –CF2– and –CF3, resulting in high aqueous dispersion. Under optimized conditions, the proposed sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity to detect TC in food samples. The limit of detection is calculated as 76 nM with a linear range of 0.4 – 10 µM. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is assigned to IFE based on the excitation spectrum of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 and the absorption spectrum of TC, as well as the compensatory fluorescence intensity of Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 with the energy absorbed by TC concentration.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are widely used in human and veterinary medicine due to their high performance in terms of their antimicrobial properties. Although given as a preventive measure, they accumulate instantly in the human body, resulting in a pernicious influence or allergic effect on human health. Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics are an important broad-spectrum antibiotic and are applied for the treatment of infectious diseases of humans and animals. Tetracycline is an antibiotic with antimicrobial properties, which is widely used in veterinary and human medicine to treat bacterial infections. Due to this special feature, TC is most frequently used in chicken, pork, cow, and aquaculture farms. The TCs are regularly used in veterinary medicine as an antibiotic and also a growth promoter. Some of the injected TCs are excreted by the excretory organs however their residues accumulate and remain in the animal body parts, which can serve as food products. Such food product consumption can lead to adverse effects on health [1]. Many countries have legally fixed maximum residue levels for various antibiotics for consumer safety. The United States Food and Drug Administration (USFDA) had established that TC maximum residue levels in milk, fat, liver, and muscle to be 0.4, 12, 6, and 2 mg/kg, respectively [2], and the European Union (EU) had established the levels in milk, egg, liver, and kidney to be 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.6 mg/kg, respectively [2], to control the levels of TCs satisfying the security value. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor their residues in foods of animal origin and so it is significant to develop effective and sensitive methods for the determination of TC residues in animal food products to ensure food safety and human health.

To determine trace level residues of TC many analytical techniques have been developed. Conventional analytical techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [4], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [5], ultra-performance chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS) [6], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [7], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [8], capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometric (CE-MS) [9] and electrochemical analysis [10], surface plasmon resonance detection [11], enzyme-linked immunoassay[12] have been applied for the determination of TC. Although these methods use well-established instrumentation, advanced procedures, and standards, with high sensitivity, they still have drawbacks like time-consuming, expensive instrumentation, complex-step extraction, matrix interference, elaborated sample pretreatment procedure, and required skilled professional operation. As a simple technique, Wang’s group suggested a colorimetric sensor using Fe3O4 for detecting TC [13]. The colorimetric method has attracted considerable interest since it is simple, fast, economical and easy operation with simple instrumentation [14]; however, the fluorescence methods have some advantages over colorimetric methods, such as high sensitivity, selectivity, and in situ real-time. They also allow for a high reading to be measured by widening the dynamic range. Furthermore, fluorescence methods are less susceptible to influences by interfering substances in comparison with colorimetric assays [15]. Among the many detection fluorescent methods, nanoparticles-based fluorescent detection is a more suitable method due to its simplicity, outstanding properties of high sensitivity, selectivity, diversity, and low cost [16–18]. Very recent research works on conventional fluorescence materials such as nanoparticles [19], quantum dots [20–23], carbon dots [24,25], and metal–organic framework [26], etc which makes fluorescence technique as a significant analysis for chemical, biological and pharmaceutical compounds.

Compared to different types of nanomaterials, perovskites are a class of hybrid materials with a variety of applications, including sensors, photodetectors, solar cells, light-emitting devices, thermoelectric devices, and lasers [27–29]. Perovskite (PRS) is a material with a crystal structure of the general formula, ABX3. In this, X is halogen or oxygen, A is a large cation that occupies the cubooctahedral site and is shared with 12 X ions. B is a small cation, fixed in an octahedral site and shared with six X anions [30–32]. Lead halide perovskite nanomaterials (LHPRS) has emerged as a new family of materials owing to their unique optoelectronic properties such as high photoluminescence quantum yields, long diffusion length, broad absorption spectra, narrow emission peak, tunability of their optical spectra throughout the visible range and long carrier lifetime [33]. These high quality optoelectronic features of LHPRS get them also in sensor applications. Though LHPRS has shown great potency, the poor stability of LHPRS to various external disruptions, including aqueous, heat, polar media, light, and oxygen, has hindered their progress in sensing applications because of its ionic nature [34,35]. Nevertheless, these issues make LHPRS are sensitive to these external disruptions and actually allow opportunities to turn this drawback into an advantage for highly sensitive sensing. To support sensing applications of LHPRS, various techniques such as chemoresistance (CR), electrochemiluminescence (ECL), photoelectrochemistry (PEC), and photoluminescence (PL) have been reported for the sensing of gases [36], metal ions [37], humidity [38,39], temperature [40], volatile organic compounds [41,42], and biomolecules [43,44].

To enhance the application of LHPRSs, some researchers have studied to improve the stability of LHPRS in polar media. Yang’s group [45] linked oxygen of polyethylene glycol to CH3NH3 + to form a shell around LHPRS in the synthetic process of CH3NH3PbI3, resulting in the improved stability of LHPRS in a humid environment. Using this PRS, they developed a salicylic sensor based on a photoelectrochemical technique. Huang’s group [46] synthesized CsPbBr3-encapsulated molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica (MIPS) for sensing 2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate, which was synthesized from CsPbBr3 on mesoporous silica by hot inject technique using (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane. Tan’s group [47] developed a pesticide sensor based on MIP, prepared by slow hydrolyzing an organosilicon monomer, CsPbBr3, into a fluorescence material. Recently, Li’s team synthesized directly CsPbBr3/Cs4PbBr6 nanoparticles in an aqueous solution based on fluorocarbon agents (FCA) [48]. However, they focused only on the influence of different fluorocarbon agents on the water stability of the LHPRS material and successfully applied the prepared LHPRS on brain tissue labeling.

IFE is a fluorescence quenching process, in which the absorption of excited or emitted light takes place through fluorescent materials called absorbers (quenchers) [49], but the absorption bands of the quenchers must have an overlapping area, which is complementary to the emission or excitation bands of the fluorophore [49–51]. Due to this fact, a limited selection of fluorescent carriers and absorbents is available to limit the application of IFE-based sensors [49–51]. However, the studies on IFE sensors have been increased because the mechanism does not have a covalent link between the absorbers and the fluorophore [52–54]. To improve the sensitivity and selectivity of analytical detection, the IFE sensor converts the absorption signal into a fluorescence signal [55], and by altering the quencher concentration the level of fluorescence quenching can be toned in the IFE [49,50].

To demonstrate the available application of LHPRS in aqueous media, the present work describes a new fluorescence sensor for the detection of TC using Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3perovskite (CPB) nanoparticles. The CPB nanoparticles with high water stability were obtained due to the presence of 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PFOS) as a water-proof layer. The fluorescence intensity of CPB with PFOS (CPB-PFOS) is reduced by the presence of TC through the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. The prepared CPB-PFOS sample was characterized with the aid of X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, FT-IR spectra, UV–Vis spectra, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and fluorescence spectra. The developed method uses CPB-PFOS materials as a fluorescence source and is simple, highly sensitive, and selective for detecting TC in aqueous solution. Moreover, the prepared CPB-PFOS material was used for the paper strip test in which the intensive color of the test paper strip can be seen by the naked eye in the presence of TC under UV light exposure. To appraise the availability of the proposed TC sensing method, the typical food samples of milk, egg, and chicken meat were used as real samples.

Lead bromide (PbBr2, 99%), oleylamine (OAm, 90%), Cesium bromide (CsBr, 99%), oleic acid (OA, 85%), hydrobromic acid (HBr, 40%), and 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PFOS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, azithromycin, ampicillin, and nitrofurantoin were purchased from Sigma. Amino acids were obtained from TCI (Japan).

The CPB-PFOS nanoparticles were synthesized by following the procedure reported by Li’s research group [48]. The precursor solutions were prepared separately from PbBr2 (0.367 g) and CsBr (0.213 g) in HBr (2 mL). CsBr solution (0.250 mL, 0.5 M) was mixed with PbBr2 solution (0.250 mL, 0.5 M) in a flask under stirring of 500 rpm, and then DMF (2 mL) was added to obtain a clear solution. The temperature of the solution was reduced to 0 °C using an ice water bath, and then PFOS (0.5 mL) was dropped into the mixture under stirring. The mixture was stirred until the color of the mixture turned green. After that, OAm (0.5 mL) and distilled water (100 mL) were poured into the emulsion, respectively, and the mixture was incubated for 4 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 7,000 rpm for 5 min and precipitation was discarded to remove unreacted PFOSand OAm.The supernatant was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rpm/ 30 min) to remove any remaining precursor solutions to obtain CPB-PFOS particles. The CPB-PFOS particles were dispersed in 15 mL distilled water as a stock solution.

The surface morphology of the synthesized nanoparticle samples was checked using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Zeiss Merlin Compact, Germany). The distribution of particle sizes of the prepared samples was estimated by measuring SEM images using the ImageJ software (N = 101). The absorption spectra of all samples were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco V-670, Japan). XRD pattern was obtained using X-ray diffractometer X’Pert PW3030/00 from PANalytical (Almelo, Netherlands). Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) measurements were carried out by using an FT-IR spectrometer (FT/IR-6300 Jasco, Japan). A fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-6500 Jasco, Japan) was used to record the fluorescent spectra of all samples. Time-resolved fluorescence study was performed using a spectrometer (MicroTime-200, Picoquant, Germany). The lifetime measurements were performed at the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI). A pulsed diode laser (single-mode/ 375 nm with a pulse width of ∼ 30 ps/ average power of ∼ 100 nW) was used as an excitation source. A dichroic mirror (Z375RDC, AHF), a long pass filter (HQ405lp, AHF), a bandpass filter (500 or 520 nm), and a single photon avalanche diode (PDM series, MPD) were used to collect emission from the samples. Time-correlated single-photon counting system (TimeHarp-260, PicoQuant GmbH, Germany) was used to count emission photons. Exponential fitting for the obtained fluorescence decay was performed using Symphotime-64 software (Ver. 2.2).

Wing-chicken meat, eggs were purchased from a traditional market in the city of Changwon, Republic of Korea. The bone chicken was removed, and then the meat was ground using a meat grinder. Five grams of meat was added to 15 mL acetonitrile and then vortex-mixed for 10 minutes. After the centrifuge, the supernatant was aspirated, and mixed with hexane (15 mL). The mixture was stirred for 15 minutes at 800 rpm and centrifuged for 10 min at 6,000 rpm. The mixture was cooled to –10 °C and the bottom layer was collected [56]. Egg sample preparation was done with a similar procedure.

The SEM image of the CPB-PFOS sample in Fig. 1 a and 1b shows the irregular shape of synthesized PRS nanoparticles with an average diameter of 20 nm caused by the simultaneous existence of the nucleation and growth process of CPB-PFOS particles during the prolonged reaction (4 h). The FT-IR spectra of the samples are displayed in Figure S1. The peaks in the 680 – 780 cm−1, 1100–1365 cm−1 band originate from C─F stretching. Peaks at 1040 cm−1, 3300 – 3400 cm−1 indicate the existence of the Si-OH group, OH groups in the prepared CPB-PFOS, respectively. The XRD pattern (Figure S2) of the prepared compound consists of major Cs4PbBr6 and minor CsPbBr3 components, where its diffraction peaks are matched with JCPDS No. 01–073-2478, and 01–075-0412, respectively, and agreed with Li’s report [48]. The presence of CsPbBr3 indicates that Cs4PbBr6 particles were converted to CsPbBr3 or CsPbBr3 nano-inclusions within the Cs4PbBr6 matrix [57,58]. The emission spectra of prepared CPB-PFOS are sharp and the position of their emission peaks does not change with different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 450 nm, which represents an arrow distribution of CPB-PFOSC particle size (Fig. 1c). The excitation spectrum with the monitor at 521 nm of the prepared CPB-PFOS is broad in the range of 250–450 nm (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1d displays the absorption spectra of the prepared CPB-PFOS and TC samples. The absorption spectrum of the prepared CPB-PFOS is broad, whereas the absorption spectrum of TC has two peaks (at 270 nm and 355 nm) and cut-off at 410 nm.

The stability of LHPRS is largely impacted by water, which originates from the dissolution or degradation of LHPRS during the fabrication process and its ionic characterization. To prevent this effect, the synthesized CPB-PFOS was protected by a fluorocarbon agent. The results of fluorescence (FL) measurements of the CPB-PFOS sample show that it has high stability in water (Figure S3). However, with the high concentration of CPB-PFOS, its FL increased slightly. This increase can be attributed to crystallization, resulting in the suppression of quenching defects [44]. This guaranteed that the prepared CPB-PFOS can be used as a water-stable fluorophore.

It was observed that the FL intensity of CPB-PFOS decreased with the presence of TC. To optimize the experimental conditions for sensing TC using CPB-PFOS, studies have been conducted on the effect of CPB-PFOS concentration and incubation time. During the optimization process, the concentration of TC was kept constant at 25 µM. Results showed that the FL intensity of the CPB-PFOS solution was reduced by 94% by using an aqueous solution of 5% (v/v) of CPB-PFOS in the presence of TC (Fig. 2 a). Studies on incubation time have shown that the addition of TC reduced the FL intensity of CPB-PFOS and obtained an equivalent value after 10 min of incubation (Figure S4).

Fig. 2b shows the response fluorescence intensity of CPB-PFOS with various concentrations of TC from 0.4 to 100 μM. The FL intensity of CPB-PFOS gradually decreased with increasing TC concentration. The results showed that excellent linearity was found in the range of 0.4 –10 μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.986 (Fig. 2c). The detection limit of TC is calculated as 76 nM based on LOD = 3σ / s (σ-the blank standard deviation and s-the slope of the linear regression equation). This value is compared with other reported values and is displayed in Table 1 .

It can be seen from Fig. 3 a that the excitation spectrum of CPB-PFOS overlaps with the absorption spectrum of TC. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of CPB-PFOS can be expected from the inner filter effect (IFE), which works excellently only if the absorption band of the absorber overlaps with the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorophore. Additionally, the average lifetime of the emission peak of the CPB-PFOS sample (∼201 ns) did not change in comparison with that of the CPB-PFOS sample in the presence of TC (as shown in Figure S5). These suggest that CPB-PFOS based IFE fluorescent assay can be used as a fluorophore suitable for TC sensing. Also, the emission peak of CPB-PFOS is located at 521 nm, which does not overlap with the absorption spectrum of TC, so the energy transfer of CPB-PFOS for TC is excluded. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture of CPB-PFOS and TC are displayed as a mathematical sum of the two components, so static quenching is also not expected. Further, the FL intensity change of CPB-PFOS was negligible (∼4.4%) in the presence of TC (25 µM) when the CPB-PFOS sample was excited by a wavelength of 450 nm. Therefore, the impact of IFE on the quenching of CPB-PFOS can be considered as a major component of the mechanism.

In IFE, the excitation light of fluorophore is spatially absorbed by the analyte, so compensation is required to obtain a linear relationship between the FL intensity of fluorophore and its concentration. The corrected FL intensity is calculated approximately and is expressed by the following equation [65,66]:
F
cor
F
obs
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
.
s
A
em
10
g
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

where, Fobs is the calculated maximum fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected maximum fluorescence intensity after removing the IFE from Fobs; Aex and Aem represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 355 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λem = 520 nm), respectively; d is the width of the cuvette (1 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the curve (0.4 cm), and s is the thickness of excitation beam (0.10 cm) [49]. The corrected fluorescence intensities at each different concentration of TC are given in Table S1. The data in Table S1 show that about 10% of the quenching effects come from other mechanisms; however, it is small and insignificant. The quenching efficiency of the corrected and observed fluorescence intensity of TC is determined after the removal of IFE from the observed suppressed fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 3b. The quenching effect is found to be mainly originated from the IFE of TC.

With respect to the determination of TC, fluorescent sensors should have both high sensitivity and good selectivity. To evaluate these properties, several confounding studies have been performed using CPB-PFOS containing 10-4 M of possible interfering agents including L–arginine, methionine, cysteine, β–alanine, aspartic acid, glutamic acid, proline, and glycine. Besides that, the influence of common ions, such as Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, F-, and SO4 2-, on the FL intensity of CPB-PFOS were also evaluated. The results showed that these interferences did not affect the FL intensity of the CPB-PFOS sensor as shown in Fig. 4 a and 4b.

To estimate the selectivity of the proposed method, other antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin, ampicillin, azithromycin, and nitrofurantoin were also tested. The response signal indicated that the FL sensor was built with high selectivity for TC determination, as shown in Fig. 4c. As shown in Fig. 4d, the absorption maximums of ciprofloxacin, ampicillin, and azithromycin appear at 230 nm and cut-off at 300 nm, while the absorption spectrum of TC is broad with a strong peak at 355 nm over a large range of 200 – 400 nm. Although the absorption spectrum of nitrofurantoin is broad over the range of 200 – 400 nm, a strong peak is seen at 312 nm and the absorbance at 355 nm is less than TC. These led to a strong inner filter effect at 355 nm for TC compared to others, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence of CPB-PFOS by TC. These results demonstrate that PRS can be used as a potential optosensor for detecting TC.

To study the applicability of the sensor in dealing with real food samples, the prepared CPB-PFOS material was tried on milk, egg, and chicken meat samples with the method of standard addition. The standard TC solution was used for estimating the recovery efficiency. The results are presented in Table 2 , which demonstrates the availability of the developed method for detecting TC in real samples.

Besides using fluorescence spectroscopy to determine TC in real samples, a paper strip test was performed on milk samples for sensing TC. Circles with a diameter of 8 mm were printed on Whatman filter paper grade #1 by a Xerox ColorQube 8570DN. The wax on the paper was melted and penetrated the paper after heating for 10 min at 60 °C. The circles were filled with 30 µL of the mixture of prepared CPB-PFOS (1 mL) and polyethylene oxide aqueous solution (6.0 mg/ 2 mL). Circles filled with sensor material were dried at room temperature and used as a visual probe for TC. Milk samples (20 µL) diluted 100 times with distilled water are mixed with TC standard solutions of different concentrations and poured into circles. The color of the circles was gradually deactivated from low to high TC concentration and can be seen with the naked eye under a UV lamp (355 nm) as shown in Fig. 5 . These preliminary results demonstrated that the prepared paper strip test could be used as a visual paper-based sensor with rapid response, economic efficiency, and strong capability for recognizing TC in food or drug analysis.

The fluorescence intensity of CPB-PFOS was quenched in the presence of TC based on the inner filter effect, in which the excitation spectrum of CPB-PFOS overlapped with the absorption spectrum of TC. This mechanism is confirmed by calculating the compensation FL intensity of the CPB-PFOS with the energy absorbed by the TC. The developed CPB-PFOS optosensor exhibited high aqueous stability, sensitivity, and selectivity for sensing TC with a low limit of detection of 76 nM in the linear range of 0.4 – 10 µM. Moreover, the synthesized CPB-PFOS material was preliminarily tested for preparing the paper strip test. The color of the paper strip test in the presence of TC can be seen by the naked eye under UV light. Detection of TC on real samples was done with high recoveries and low RSD values.

Truong Thi Thuy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Bui The Huy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Avvaru Praveen Kumar: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Validation. Yong-Ill Lee: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2020R1A2C2007028 and NRF-2020R1I1A3A04036531). We greatly acknowledge Dr. Weon-Sik Chae in Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI) for fluorescence lifetime measurements and discussion on quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2021.08.046.

C. Pizan-Aquino, A. Wong, L. Avilés-Félix, S. Khan, G. Picasso, M.D.P.T. Sotomayor, Mater. Chem. Phys., 245 (2020) 122777. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2020.122777
